titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Henry A Robinson to Robert Chalmers, 29 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Henry A. Robinson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Henry A Robinson (1857-1927) to Sir Robert Chalmers (1858-1938). The letter concerns Sir Thomas J. Stafford (1857-1935), 1st baronet, doctor and medical commissioner of the Local Government Board of Ireland, who has been unable to work due to ill health. Stafford has missed considerable periods of work (twelve months in the last four years). Robinson writes to inquire about what pay, if any, Stafford should receive due to his being absence because of a recent operation. Sir Henry Augustus Robinson was an Irish public servant. He was primarily responsible for the drafting and implementation of the local government act of 1898, and shortly before the bill became law was appointed vice-president of the Local Government Board.Robert Chalmers was the under-secretary for Ireland. Chalmers became under-secretary for a brief period following the resignation of Matthew Nathan (1862-1939) following the 1916 Rising.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/5613/9101/1

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Local Government Board, Dublin. 29th May, 1916 No. 60/M/1916. Office. Sir, I desire to bring under the notice of Their
Excellencies the Lords Justices and The Lords
Commissioners of His Majesty's Treasury the case
of Sir Thomas J. Stafford, Bart., C.B. , etc., Medical
Commissioner of the Local Government Board for
Ireland, who is at the present time absent from duty
on sick leave. During the past four years St Thomas Stafford,
owing to the state of his health, has been compelled
to be absent from duty for considerable periods, and
from 1st July, 1912 to the 31st December, 1912 he
was so absent on full pay. From the 1st January, 1913
to the 31st January, 1913 he was placed on half pay,
and resumed duty on the 1st February, 1913. Later in
that year, as his health was unsatisfactory, Sir Thomas
Stafford was again unable to perform his duties, and
he was on leave of absence on half pay from the 1st
September, 1913 to the 31st January, 1914, when he again
returned to the Office. Sir Thomas Stafford had to undergo an operation
for appendicitis on the 5th April, ultimo, and, although
now convalescent, is not yet able to resume duty. As
his sick leave amounts in the aggregate to twelve months
during the past four years it is necessary, under
Article The Under Secretary,
Dublin Castle. Under Secretary
May a copy of this
letter be sent to the
Treasury for directions?
J.L.
29.V.16. Yes, giving date
of his birth,
RC 30/5/16 Copied Rd. 31/5 Date of birth-
3rd May,1857. Done & Copied
MP.
31/5
Article 19 (4) of the Order in Council of 10th January,
1910, to report the matter to Their Lordships to decide
what payment ( if any) in respect of salary shall be made
to him during the present period of sick leave. Sir Thomas Stafford entered the service as a Medical
Inspector of the Board on the 3rd February, 1890 and has
therefore completed twentysix years service, and is entitled
under the Treasury Warrant to seven added years. His
pensionable service is therefore thirty three years, and,
as he did not adopt the provisions of the Superannuation
Act, 1909, in the event of retirement, his full pension
would amount to 33/60ths of Â£1,200, his present salary as
Medical Commissioner, of which he has been in receipt
since the 5 May, 1902. I suggest for Their Lordships' consideration that
half pay, (Â£600 a year), might be allowed to the Medical
Commissioner as from the 5th April until he is able to
return to duty, which he hopes to do in a few weeks. In
the meantime his duties are discharged during his absence
by one of the Medical Inspectors. I desire to add that the Office of Medical Commissioner
is a statutory one and, in the event of Sir Thomas Stafford's
retiring from the service, a successor would have to be
appointed by the Crown. I am, Sir,
Your obedient Servant, HA Robinson
Vice President.
